query: What is the maximum capacity threshold for a  generator in MISO to be eligible for the fast-track interconnection process in MISO, according to MISO Tariff Attachment X Generator Interconnection Procedures (GIP)? Cite the relevant source report and section for your answer
final_answer:
## Maximum Capacity Threshold for MISO Fast-Track Interconnection

Under MISO's Attachment X Generator Interconnection Procedures (GIP), the maximum capacity threshold for a generator to be eligible for the **Fast Track Process** (Section 14) is **5 MW** (aggregate net Generating Facility Capacity).

### Source Citation

The FERC order in **Docket No. ER26-677-000**, issued **February 3, 2026** (194 FERC ¶ 61,093), states at **footnote 3**:

> "MISO's GIP defines small generating facility to 'mean a Generating Facility that has an aggregate net Generating Facility Capacity of **no more than five MW** and meets the requirements of **Section 14 [FAST TRACK PROCESS.]** and Appendix 3 [CERTIFICATION CODES AND STANDARDS AND CERTIFICATION OF SMALL GENERATOR EQUIPMENT PACKAGES].'" — *MISO, Tariff, attach. X (GIP) (182.0.0) § 1 (Definitions).*

**Source URL:** [FERC Order, Docket No. ER26-677-000 (PDF)](https://cdn.misoenergy.org/2026-02-03_194%20FERC%20%C2%B6%2061%2C093_Docket%20No.%20ER26-677-000739667.pdf)

### Additional Confirmation

- The **MISO Dashboard** page titled "GIA Small Generator Insurance Requirements 5 MW and Under" explicitly references "small generators **5 MW and under that are Fast Track eligible**." ([Source](https://www.misoenergy.org/engage/MISO-Dashboard/gia-small-generator-insurance-requirements-5-mw-and-under/))

- The **MISO GIP Appendix 1** (Interconnection Request form) includes a checkbox option for "**Fast Track Process for Small Generating Facility**," and Section 2.1(b) of the GIP states that "Section 14 of the GIP applies to a request to interconnect a certified Small Generating Facility meeting the certification criteria set forth in Appendix 3." ([Source](https://cdn.misoenergy.org/Attachment%20X%20-%20GIP%20(Effective%20January%2031%202025)330116.pdf))

### Summary

| Criterion | Threshold |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Maximum aggregate net Generating Facility Capacity** | **≤ 5 MW** |
| **Governing Tariff Section** | **Attachment X, Section 14 (Fast Track Process)** |
| **Additional Requirement** | Must meet certification criteria in Appendix 3 |
| **Initial Review Timeline** | 15 Business Days |

Generators exceeding 5 MW must go through the standard Definitive Planning Phase (DPP) interconnection process under Sections 2–13 of the GIP.

      "url": "https://www.renewableenergyworld.com/solar/utility-scale/fast-track-process-is-available-for-renewable-projects-under-5-mw-at-miso-and-spp/",

      "author": "Rao Konidena",
      "published_date": "2021-01-28T17:54:53.000Z",
      "text": "Fast Track Process is available for renewable projects under 5 MW at MISO and SPP\n\nWith hundreds of renewable projects sitting in the regional grid operator interconnection queue, developers should investigate the fast track process at Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO) and Southwest Power Pool (SPP) for projects less than 5 MW in capacity. There are similarities and differences in both MISO and SPP fast track processes. Both processes need study deposits and provide an initial review with a customer options meeting. Even with a straightforward process like fast track, there are very few MISO and SPP projects in this sub-5-MW category. BackgroundLarge generator interconnection projects are sitting in the queue waiting to be studied. If the MISO peak load is 127,000 MW, the entire MISO interconnection queue currently has 91,000 MW in different queue stages. The same holds for SPP– SPP’s peak demand is 55,000 MW, and its interconnection queue has 85,000 MW of projects. Out of those 85 gigawatts (GWs), more than 40% are solar projects. For utility-scale renewable projects, it makes sense for ISO interconnection engineers to run feasibility, impact, and facility studies separately. But for projects less than or equal to 5 MW, transmission providers like MISO or SPP’s role is minimum. The primary role is of the Transmission Owner (TO) or the distribution utility, as the case might be. But the renewable developer must go through the transmission provider because of capacity rights. Going through the interconnection queue affords transmission access for smaller renewable project developers, who can then contract with the load-serving entities, to participate in energy and capacity markets. There are 3 similarities in MISO and SPP Fast Track ProcessBoth MISO and SPP generator interconnection tariff section 14 discusses the fast track process containing 4 sub-sections – 1) Applicability, 2) Initial Review, 3) Customer Options Meeting, and 4) Supplemental Review. Once the renewable project meets the eligibility criteria, the initial review looks at the detailed interconnection requirements, such as, if the project connects to a radial distribution circuit, then the project should not cause an excess of 15% of the line section annual peak load at the substation. At both ISOs, the supplemental review entails a minimum load screen, voltage plus power quality screen, and a safety and reliability screen. Both MISO and SPP processes need deposit fees. At MISO, it is $5000, and at SPP, it is $1,000. Even with lower deposit fees at SPP, SPP staff said they have limited experience processing fast track projects, due to the process being largely underutilized. At MISO, there are only 2 solar projects active in the queue with a capacity of less than 5 MW. There are a couple of diesel projects under “active” study status in the MISO queue. While the initial study deposit fees are less at both ISOs, there may be additional fees when the TO does supplemental review. That is dependent on the interconnecting TO. It appears those fees would be negotiated during the customer options meeting. There are 3 main differencesSPP applicability criteria appear to be more centered around voltage class. Projects less than 5 kV and less than 500 kW are the first category for fast track eligibility. There are voltage class categories such as less than 15 kV, 30 kV, and 69 kV in the SPP process. SPP explicitly states that any project connecting to transmission lines greater than 69 kV would not qualify for the fast track process. But MISO process only discusses 5 MW as the threshold for fast track process applicability. Another difference in MISO and SPP markets is that MISO has a Planning Reserve Auction (PRA) where investor-owned utilities can point to small renewable projects and address their reserve margin requirements. At the last MISO auction, 850 MW of solar cleared MISO capacity auction. Moreover, MISO has Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) approval to treat solar like a dispatchable resource (like wind and other fuels). This approval means solar units should submit their production forecasts to the MISO operator, but there is no financial penalty for deviations. The final difference between the MISO and SPP fast track process is that the SPP process has an environmental review section. Since SPP has a federal power agency as a transmission owning member – Western Area Power Administration (WAPA) – Upper Great Plains (UGP) region(called Western-UGP in SPP tariff), an environmental review applies to projects seeking interconnection to the transmission facilities of Western UGP. ConclusionIt is no secret that renewable projects that entered the interconnection queue in 2016 are seeing their final set of upgrade cost reports now. We can expect new renewable projects are entering the queue in 2021 to see their study results at least mid-2024. But this delay should not deter small renewable developers.\n\nThere is a fast track process for project sizes less than 5 MW with the entire process takes less than 90 days. Yes, the criteria to qualify for fast track is detailed, but if the project qualifies – renewable developers would be better served to take this step towards connecting to the wholesale markets.\n\n## Recommended For You\n\n---\n\n### Green light for green energy: Cypress Creek secures $3.5B for Arkansas solar and battery storage megaproject\n\n### US automaker implores utilities to explore V2G, makes big bet on the grid battery of the future\n\n### The first vertically integrated solar facility in the U.S. is now in operation\n\n### Green light for green energy: Cypress Creek secures $3.5B for Arkansas solar and battery storage megaproject\n\nCypress Creek Energy announced a $3.5 billion financial close for Phases 1 and 2 of the Steel River Energy Center in Arkansas.\n\n5 min read\n\n### The NERC Category 2 Deadline Has Passed. Now What?\n\nThe May 15, 2026 NERC Category 2 deadline marked a major shift for inverter-based resources across North America. Many solar, wind, battery storage, and hybrid assets that were previously outside direct NERC oversight are now subject to new registration and compliance expectations.       "author": "Rao Konidena",
      "published_date": "2026-06-12T20:01:13.000Z",
      "text": "Why don’t we see more energy storage interconnected at MISO?\n\n# Rao’s Newsletter\n\nSubscribeSign in\n\n# Why don’t we see more energy storage interconnected at MISO?\n\n### Why don't other MISO states follow Michigan and Illinois's lead on storage?\n\nJun 12, 2026\n\n2\n\nShare\n\nA single state ISO like California ISO has more energy storage interconnected than a multi-state MISO. Why?\n\nCalifornia ISO has more utility-connected storage than behind the meter storage. As of July 31, 2025, based on the California Energy Commission website, California has 13,880 MW of utility storage, 2,213 MW of residential storage and 849 MW of commercial storage, leading to a total of 16,942 MW of storage in California.\n\nIs that because most states in MISO are vertically integrated? Meaning, the investor-owned utilities own generation, transmission and distribution – is that a barrier for energy storage adoption? Compared to PJM, where most states restructured and separated generation from the wires business, so capacity is procured through a competitive market rather than owned by the distribution utility. Based on California’s 13,880 MW of utility storage, one would expect as a floor a multi-state MISO would have at least that much utility storage capacity. Notably, California shares this restructured, non-vertically-integrated structure with PJM — which is itself a clue that market structure, not state size, may be doing the work here.\n\nBut MISO has strikingly little. Based on MISO’s real-time market offer data for March 14, 2026, only 18 storage resources offered into the market, totaling roughly 1,096 MW of capacity (about 4,372 MWh, or an average four-hour duration). [Source: MISO real-time offer data, 3/14/2026]\n\nSource: Claude, based on MISO Real-Time Offer data for 3/14/26\n\nIf we blow up the eight projects under 20 MW, the size distinction is stark: six of the eight are 5 MW or smaller. That threshold is not an accident. Under Section 14 of MISO's Attachment X, a Small Generating Facility no larger than 5 MW qualifies for the Fast Track Process — a 15-business-day initial review instead of the multi-year Definitive Planning Phase queue. The 5 MW line is a real regulatory breakpoint, and developers size to it.\n\nSource: Claude, based on MISO Real-Time Offer data for 3/14/26\n\nIs that because most MISO states do not have an energy storage mandate or a goal? Michigan’s goal is 2,500 MW by 2030 (SB 271), and while installed capacity was minimal as of early 2025, the Michigan PSC has since approved over 1,300 MW of storage contracts in a single 2026 order — showing what a mandate plus active commission follow-through can unlock. Illinois, under its January 2026 Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act, directs utilities to install 3,000 MW by 2030. The goals beg the question: why don’t other MISO states have similar mandates?\n\nIs that because California’s retail prices are so high on average that residential battery storage capacity took off, but that’s not the same across MISO states since they have lower average residential prices? California has 33 cents per kWh on average for residential compared to Minnesota’s 15 cents per kWh, according to this website. The MISO states – Michigan and Illinois, that have energy storage procurement targets have 20 cents per kWh and 18 cents per kWh for residential sector, respectively. So, is that what it takes for other states to adopt energy storage procurement targets – a high residential bill? But Wisconsin has almost 19 cents per kWh, so why doesn’t it have a procurement target for storage?\n\nIs that because energy storage has so many attributes that most MISO states have been unable to pick one? One of the benefits of energy storage is that it reduces the transmission access charges, if interconnected at transmission. Since transmission-interconnected storage can reduce these access charges, the transmission pricing zones with the highest and fastest-rising charges should be the most attractive for storage — yet they aren’t.\n\nIf you look at MISO Rates for Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) Schedule 9 Network charges for Jan 2026, posted on MISO OASIS, the highest rates are in ITC Midwest and ATCLLC Wisconsin zones – more than $100,000 per MW-YR. ITC Midwest LLC has $165,900 per MW-YR. In Jan 2021, ITC Midwest LLC had $131,700 per MW-YR. So, in 5 years, ITC Midwest LLC’s transmission charges have increased by 26% - more than 5% per year. This begs the question – why don’t we see more storage in MISO transmission zones that have highest increases over the past 5 years? CAISO rates are much lower compared to MISO’s.\n\nSo, why don’t we see more energy storage interconnected at MISO? The honest answer is likely all of the above at once — but the one lever a commission directly controls is the mandate, which is exactly the variable most MISO states have left untouched.\n\nBut a mandate alone isn't enough — a state can set a target and still see nothing get built if the regulatory plumbing isn't there. In a new white paper, Making Distributed Storage Work in Your State, my co-authors Kerinia Cusick and Mrinmayee Kale and I lay out the four roadblocks state commissions have to clear for third-party storage to actually reach the distribution grid: counting BESS as a peak-reduction resource in distribution planning, building retail tariffs that define both charging costs and compensation, preventing storage and new load from competing for the same substation capacity, and standardizing communication requirements instead of letting each utility invent its own. The mandate sets the goal; these four fix the path.\n\n2\n\nShare\n\n#### Discussion about this post\n\nCommentsRestacks\n\nNo posts\n\n### Ready for more?\n\nSubscribe\n\n© 2026 Rao Konidena · Privacy∙ Terms∙ Collection notice\n\nStart your Substack Get the app\n\nSubstack is the home for great culture",

      "url": "https://www.misoenergy.org/planning/resource-utilization/generator-interconnection/",
      "title": "Generator Interconnection",
      "text": "Generator Interconnection\n\nSkip to main content Skip to footer\n\nPlanningResource Utilization Generator Interconnection\n\nFollow\n\nGenerator Interconnection and Retirement\n\nMISO’s generator interconnection process vets and approves the addition of new energy sources into the MISO-controlled transmission network. It also manages generation retirement decisions to ensure that there’s enough incoming energy to replace what is phased out. It’s a non-discriminatory access system, open to generators within the MISO territory.\n\nA successful application will result in an interconnection agreement that allows a connection to the MISO grid.\n\n#### Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS)\n\nFuture capacity forecasts highlight the urgent need to accelerate new generation additions and streamline interconnection studies. MISO’s Generation Interconnection Queue (GIQ) Cap, Phase 1 automation, and January 2024 reforms—alongside Order 2023 compliance—are expected to improve queue management, though full timeline reductions may take several years.\n\nThe Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS) is a targeted, temporary process designed to issue a Generation Interconnection Agreement in three months. It enables reliability-critical resources to come online quickly while broader queue processing timelines are being reduced.\n\nMISO accepts ERAS applications on a rolling basis. The ERAS application portal will remain open until either MISO has studied 68 ERAS Interconnection Requests or until application deadline for the last cycle (May 10, 2027) — whichever comes first. To be considered for a cycle, the ERAS application must be submitted at least 15 Business Days prior to cycle kickoff. In accordance with the Tariff, the order in which projects are studied is determined by submission time, the resolution of any application deficiencies, and whether projects affect common constraints.\n\nERAS Informational Guide| Sample RERRA Verification Form for New Load Additions| Sample RERRA Verification Form for Resource Adequacy Deficiency| Offtake Agreements Documentation| ERAS Submission Metrics| ERAS Interconnection Requests (xlsx)| ERAS Interconnection Requests (pdf)|\n\nERAS Cycle Kickoff Dates\n\n| 2025 | 2026 | 2027 |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Quarter 1 | Cycle 3: 3/2 | Cycle 7: 3/1 |\n| Quarter 2 | Cycle 4: 6/1 | Cycle 8: 6/1 |\n| Quarter 3 | Cycle 1: 9/2 | Cycle 5: 9/1 |\n| Quarter 4 | Cycle 2: 12/1 | Cycle 6: 12/1 |\n\n#### Application Process\n\n### Pre-Queue\n\nThe Pre-Queue phase includes an overview of the process, timeline and expectations pertaining to the output of the Generator Interconnection process. Customers may submit an application at the end of this phase.\n\nMISO offers regular information sessions to ensure the customer understands queue process and requirements in every phase of the process. Interconnection customers may request an ad-hoc session to discuss potential projects with MISO and the associated transmission owner.\n\nNote: Generator Interconnection Queue applications are accepted in alignment with the existing study cycle. Applications received after the posted study cycle deadline are assigned to the next cycle. MISO charges a flat, non-refundable D1 application fee (currently $7,600). Additionally, a D2 deposit is required to fund the three Definitive Planning Phase System Impact Studies and the Facilities Study.\n\n### Application\n\nCustomers work through an online application, located on the Generator Interconnection Online Portal. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY\n\nThe energy industry is changing rapidly. Urgent resource adequacy and reliability challenges are arising because of continued electrification efforts, a resurgence in manufacturing, and an unexpected demand for energy-hungry data centers to support artificial intelligence, the accelerated early retirement of generation, and the growing preference for low or no carbon emission resources. These factors combined produce rising resource adequacy and reliability concerns across the country and a complex policy landscape.       "text": "PAC: ERAS Proposal (PAC-2023-1) (20250219)\n\nSkip to main content Skip to footer\n\n# PAC: ERAS Proposal (PAC-2023-1) (20250219)\n\nItem Expired\n\n##### Related Entity(s):\n\nPlanning Advisory Committee (PAC)\n\n##### Topic(s):\n\nGenerator Interconnection\n\n---\n\nIn the February 19, 2025, meeting of the Planning Advisory Committee (PAC), MISO presented the latest Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS) proposal.       "text": "Fast Track Process is available for renewable projects under 5 MW at MISO and SPP\n\nWith hundreds of renewable projects sitting in the regional grid operator interconnection queue, developers should investigate the fast track process at Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO) and Southwest Power Pool (SPP) for projects less than 5 MW in capacity. There are similarities and differences in both MISO and SPP fast track processes. Both processes need study deposits and provide an initial review with a customer options meeting. Even with a straightforward process like fast track, there are very few MISO and SPP projects in this sub-5-MW category. BackgroundLarge generator interconnection projects are sitting in the queue waiting to be studied. If the MISO peak load is 127,000 MW, the entire MISO interconnection queue currently has 91,000 MW in different queue stages. The same holds for SPP– SPP’s peak demand is 55,000 MW, and its interconnection queue has 85,000 MW of projects. Out of those 85 gigawatts (GWs), more than 40% are solar projects. For utility-scale renewable projects, it makes sense for ISO interconnection engineers to run feasibility, impact, and facility studies separately. But for projects less than or equal to 5 MW, transmission providers like MISO or SPP’s role is minimum. The primary role is of the Transmission Owner (TO) or the distribution utility, as the case might be. But the renewable developer must go through the transmission provider because of capacity rights. Going through the interconnection queue affords transmission access for smaller renewable project developers, who can then contract with the load-serving entities, to participate in energy and capacity markets. There are 3 similarities in MISO and SPP Fast Track ProcessBoth MISO and SPP generator interconnection tariff section 14 discusses the fast track process containing 4 sub-sections – 1) Applicability, 2) Initial Review, 3) Customer Options Meeting, and 4) Supplemental Review. Once the renewable project meets the eligibility criteria, the initial review looks at the detailed interconnection requirements, such as, if the project connects to a radial distribution circuit, then the project should not cause an excess of 15% of the line section annual peak load at the substation. At both ISOs, the supplemental review entails a minimum load screen, voltage plus power quality screen, and a safety and reliability screen. Both MISO and SPP processes need deposit fees. At MISO, it is $5000, and at SPP, it is $1,000. Even with lower deposit fees at SPP, SPP staff said they have limited experience processing fast track projects, due to the process being largely underutilized. At MISO, there are only 2 solar projects active in the queue with a capacity of less than 5 MW. There are a couple of diesel projects under “active” study status in the MISO queue. While the initial study deposit fees are less at both ISOs, there may be additional fees when the TO does supplemental review. That is dependent on the interconnecting TO. It appears those fees would be negotiated during the customer options meeting. There are 3 main differencesSPP applicability criteria appear to be more centered around voltage class. Projects less than 5 kV and less than 500 kW are the first category for fast track eligibility. There are voltage class categories such as less than 15 kV, 30 kV, and 69 kV in the SPP process. SPP explicitly states that any project connecting to transmission lines greater than 69 kV would not qualify for the fast track process. But MISO process only discusses 5 MW as the threshold for fast track process applicability. Another difference in MISO and SPP markets is that MISO has a Planning Reserve Auction (PRA) where investor-owned utilities can point to small renewable projects and address their reserve margin requirements. At the last MISO auction, 850 MW of solar cleared MISO capacity auction. Moreover, MISO has Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) approval to treat solar like a dispatchable resource (like wind and other fuels). This approval means solar units should submit their production forecasts to the MISO operator, but there is no financial penalty for deviations. The final difference between the MISO and SPP fast track process is that the SPP process has an environmental review section. Since SPP has a federal power agency as a transmission owning member – Western Area Power Administration (WAPA) – Upper Great Plains (UGP) region(called Western-UGP in SPP tariff), an environmental review applies to projects seeking interconnection to the transmission facilities of Western UGP. ConclusionIt is no secret that renewable projects that entered the interconnection queue in 2016 are seeing their final set of upgrade cost reports now. We can expect new renewable projects are entering the queue in 2021 to see their study results at least mid-2024. But this delay should not deter small renewable developers.\n\nThere is a fast track process for project sizes less than 5 MW with the entire process takes less than 90 days. Yes, the criteria to qualify for fast track is detailed, but if the project qualifies – renewable developers would be better served to take this step towards connecting to the wholesale markets.\n\n## Recommended For You\n\n---\n\n### Green light for green energy: Cypress Creek secures $3.5B for Arkansas solar and battery storage megaproject\n\n### US automaker implores utilities to explore V2G, makes big bet on the grid battery of the future\n\n### The first vertically integrated solar facility in the U.S. is now in operation\n\n### Green light for green energy: Cypress Creek secures $3.5B for Arkansas solar and battery storage megaproject\n\nCypress Creek Energy announced a $3.5 billion financial close for Phases 1 and 2 of the Steel River Energy Center in Arkansas.\n\n5 min read\n\n### The NERC Category 2 Deadline Has Passed.       "text": "Why don’t we see more energy storage interconnected at MISO?\n\n# Rao’s Newsletter\n\nSubscribeSign in\n\n# Why don’t we see more energy storage interconnected at MISO?\n\n### Why don't other MISO states follow Michigan and Illinois's lead on storage?\n\nJun 12, 2026\n\n2\n\nShare\n\nA single state ISO like California ISO has more energy storage interconnected than a multi-state MISO. Why?\n\nCalifornia ISO has more utility-connected storage than behind the meter storage. As of July 31, 2025, based on the California Energy Commission website, California has 13,880 MW of utility storage, 2,213 MW of residential storage and 849 MW of commercial storage, leading to a total of 16,942 MW of storage in California.\n\nIs that because most states in MISO are vertically integrated? Meaning, the investor-owned utilities own generation, transmission and distribution – is that a barrier for energy storage adoption? Compared to PJM, where most states restructured and separated generation from the wires business, so capacity is procured through a competitive market rather than owned by the distribution utility. Based on California’s 13,880 MW of utility storage, one would expect as a floor a multi-state MISO would have at least that much utility storage capacity. Notably, California shares this restructured, non-vertically-integrated structure with PJM — which is itself a clue that market structure, not state size, may be doing the work here.\n\nBut MISO has strikingly little. Based on MISO’s real-time market offer data for March 14, 2026, only 18 storage resources offered into the market, totaling roughly 1,096 MW of capacity (about 4,372 MWh, or an average four-hour duration). [Source: MISO real-time offer data, 3/14/2026]\n\nSource: Claude, based on MISO Real-Time Offer data for 3/14/26\n\nIf we blow up the eight projects under 20 MW, the size distinction is stark: six of the eight are 5 MW or smaller. That threshold is not an accident. Under Section 14 of MISO's Attachment X, a Small Generating Facility no larger than 5 MW qualifies for the Fast Track Process — a 15-business-day initial review instead of the multi-year Definitive Planning Phase queue. The 5 MW line is a real regulatory breakpoint, and developers size to it.\n\nSource: Claude, based on MISO Real-Time Offer data for 3/14/26\n\nIs that because most MISO states do not have an energy storage mandate or a goal? Michigan’s goal is 2,500 MW by 2030 (SB 271), and while installed capacity was minimal as of early 2025, the Michigan PSC has since approved over 1,300 MW of storage contracts in a single 2026 order — showing what a mandate plus active commission follow-through can unlock. Illinois, under its January 2026 Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act, directs utilities to install 3,000 MW by 2030. steps.content:
## Maximum Capacity Threshold for MISO Fast-Track Interconnection

Under MISO's Attachment X Generator Interconnection Procedures (GIP), the maximum capacity threshold for a generator to be eligible for the **Fast Track Process** (Section 14) is **5 MW** (aggregate net Generating Facility Capacity).

### Source Citation

The FERC order in **Docket No. ER26-677-000**, issued **February 3, 2026** (194 FERC ¶ 61,093), states at **footnote 3**:

> "MISO's GIP defines small generating facility to 'mean a Generating Facility that has an aggregate net Generating Facility Capacity of **no more than five MW** and meets the requirements of **Section 14 [FAST TRACK PROCESS.]** and Appendix 3 [CERTIFICATION CODES AND STANDARDS AND CERTIFICATION OF SMALL GENERATOR EQUIPMENT PACKAGES].'" — *MISO, Tariff, attach. X (GIP) (182.0.0) § 1 (Definitions).*

**Source URL:** [FERC Order, Docket No. ER26-677-000 (PDF)](https://cdn.misoenergy.org/2026-02-03_194%20FERC%20%C2%B6%2061%2C093_Docket%20No.%20ER26-677-000739667.pdf)

### Additional Confirmation

- The **MISO Dashboard** page titled "GIA Small Generator Insurance Requirements 5 MW and Under" explicitly references "small generators **5 MW and under that are Fast Track eligible**." ([Source](https://www.misoenergy.org/engage/MISO-Dashboard/gia-small-generator-insurance-requirements-5-mw-and-under/))

- The **MISO GIP Appendix 1** (Interconnection Request form) includes a checkbox option for "**Fast Track Process for Small Generating Facility**," and Section 2.1(b) of the GIP states that "Section 14 of the GIP applies to a request to interconnect a certified Small Generating Facility meeting the certification criteria set forth in Appendix 3." ([Source](https://cdn.misoenergy.org/Attachment%20X%20-%20GIP%20(Effective%20January%2031%202025)330116.pdf))

### Summary

| Criterion | Threshold |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Maximum aggregate net Generating Facility Capacity** | **≤ 5 MW** |
| **Governing Tariff Section** | **Attachment X, Section 14 (Fast Track Process)** |
| **Additional Requirement** | Must meet certification criteria in Appendix 3 |
| **Initial Review Timeline** | 15 Business Days |

Generators exceeding 5 MW must go through the standard Definitive Planning Phase (DPP) interconnection process under Sections 2–13 of the GIP.

So the answer is clearly 5 MW. The fast-track process under Section 14 of MISO's Attachment X GIP applies to Small Generating Facilities with an aggregate net generating facility capacity of no more than 5 MW (i.e., ≤ 5 MW).

The key sources are:
